refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, reusable form. Atomicity is achieved by deferring the specific feature types (terrain, obstacles, zones) to sub-FRs.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per the acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, restricted zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and version the worksite model for access by planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured geometric model (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with semantic labels and metadata; expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign each zone a feature inventory (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as a graph of zone nodes with edge constraints; persist in a queryable schema.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a rule-based symbolic representation (e.g., logic rules or ontology) that encodes spatial relationships, safety constraints, and operational policies; derive a queryable model on demand by evaluating rules against sensor observations.

DP4: Establish a real-time streaming pipeline that continuously ingests sensor data, applies feature extraction and classification, and maintains an incrementally updated spatial model; expose model state via subscription/polling interface for planning/execution.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to a hybrid approach: use pre-defined templates (e.g., site layout blueprints, CAD models) as a baseline; augment with live sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features; merge into a composite model.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid baseline-augmented model. Establish a composite spatial representation by anchoring to pre-defined worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, or prior maps) and augmenting with live multi-modal sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features. Fuse baseline and live data into a unified, queryable geometric and semantic model. Expose model state and feature queries via a standardized interface for planning and execution consumers. Maintain model freshness and consistency through incremental updates and conflict resolution policies aligned with safety and operational constraints.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor modality selection and feature extraction logic—mitigated by abstracting sensor input as a generic observation stream and keeping extraction logic modular.

Dependency on baseline template availability and accuracy—mitigated by graceful degradation: if baseline unavailable, fall back to DP1 (pure sensor fusion); if baseline inaccurate, detection of deviations triggers re-calibration.
Coupling between model update frequency and planning/execution cycle time—mitigated by decoupling via asynchronous query interface; consumers poll or subscribe at their own cadence.
Potential coupling to specific safety/operational policy encoding—mitigated by externalizing policies as queryable constraints rather than hard-coding into model structure.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves loose coupling by separating concerns: baseline provision (external), live sensing (pluggable), fusion logic (centralized), and policy enforcement (declarative). The hybrid approach reduces brittleness compared to pure sensor fusion (DP1) or pure symbolic reasoning (DP3), and supports incremental model refinement without full re-acquisition.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite baseline (blueprint, CAD, or prior map) is available and reasonably accurate at mission start. RISK: If baseline is stale, missing, or significantly inaccurate, fusion may produce misleading composite model. MITIGATION: Implement baseline validation checks; flag high-confidence deviations; support manual override.

ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor suite (vision, range, inertial) is available and calibrated. RISK: Sensor failures, miscalibration, or occlusions degrade feature detection. MITIGATION: Implement sensor health monitoring; use redundancy where safety-critical; graceful degradation to available modalities.

ASSUMPTION: Feature extraction and semantic labeling can be automated or semi-automated. RISK: Misclassification of hazards or features leads to unsafe planning. MITIGATION: Conservative labeling (over-report hazards); human-in-the-loop validation for high-risk features; traceability of labeling confidence.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formalized and queryable. RISK: Policies are ambiguous, incomplete, or conflict with model representation. MITIGATION: Explicit policy schema; validation against model; escalation for unresolvable conflicts.

ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model latency and eventual consistency. RISK: Stale model leads to unsafe or inefficient decisions. MITIGATION: Define acceptable model age; implement update prioritization; alert consumers to model freshness.

ASSUMPTION: Composite model can be efficiently queried and updated in real-time. RISK: Model complexity or update frequency exceeds computational budget. MITIGATION: Hierarchical model representation; lazy evaluation; caching; spatial indexing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly separate three sub-concerns: (1) Baseline provisioning and validation (DP5a), (2) Live feature detection and anomaly flagging (DP5b), and (3) Fusion, conflict resolution, and query interface (DP5c). This decomposition clarifies ownership boundaries (IM layer) and allows independent evolution of baseline sources, sensor pipelines, and model consumers. Additionally, introduce a formal model schema (e.g., GeoJSON + semantic tags + policy constraints) to ensure interoperability and auditability. Define acceptance criteria for model freshness, feature detection recall/precision, and policy compliance.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial representation engine that maintains hybrid baseline-augmented models, fuses multi-modal sensor data with pre-defined worksite templates, detects deviations and dynamic hazards, and exposes unified geometric/semantic queries to planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests live multi-modal sensor observations (LiDAR, camera, thermal, etc.) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for real-time augmentation of baseline model.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Exposes queryable model state and feature queries (geometric, semantic, hazard, deviation) to planning and execution consumers for mission adaptation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Publishes detected deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features to Safety Services for constraint-aligned conflict resolution and safety enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronizes baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) and maintains model freshness through incremental updates and consistency policies across distributed nodes.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE is the natural locus for spatial reasoning, sensor fusion, and model management. Baseline template anchoring, live augmentation, deviation detection, and unified query exposure are core NGHE responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (Vehicle/Teleoperation) and safety constraint enforcement (Safety Services) cross boundary.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a platform for actuation and sensor mounting, not a reasoning engine. Placing hybrid model logic here would scatter spatial representation across vehicle instances, breaking unified model coherence and forcing expensive synchronization.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface layer, not a persistent spatial reasoning system. Model fusion, baseline anchoring, and conflict resolution belong in a stateful, autonomous reasoning module, not in an operator-facing control interface.

Weak fit. Safety Services enforces constraints and policies on model outputs, but does not own the model itself. Placing hybrid model logic here conflates constraint enforcement with spatial representation, violating separation of concerns and making model reuse across safety contexts difficult.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of the hybrid baseline-augmented spatial model. It ingests live telemetry via Telemetry interface, anchors to baseline templates via Network Link, exposes queryable state via Tasking interface, and publishes hazard/deviation alerts via SafetyAlert interface. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert outputs to enforce constraints; Vehicle and Teleoperation consume Tasking queries for planning and execution. This architecture centralizes model coherence, eliminates duplication, and enables consistent conflict resolution.
containment_risks: NGHE Solution becomes a critical bottleneck if model update latency or query throughput is insufficient for real-time planning/execution loops. Mitigation: design incremental update and caching strategies to meet latency SLAs.

Baseline template synchronization across distributed NGHE instances (if deployed redundantly) requires strong consistency policies. Mitigation: define explicit conflict resolution rules aligned with safety constraints and operational priorities.

Telemetry ingestion rate and sensor fusion complexity may exceed NGHE computational capacity in high-bandwidth scenarios. Mitigation: implement sensor filtering, decimation, and edge preprocessing in Vehicle/Teleoperation to reduce NGHE load.

Safety Services must react to SafetyAlert outputs in real-time; delays in model updates or alert propagation could compromise safety. Mitigation: establish hard real-time guarantees for hazard detection and alert latency.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criteria explicitly require 'worksite model/map generated and available to planning/execution functions for agreed set of site features.' NGHE Solution's Tasking interface directly satisfies this by exposing queryable geometric/semantic model state. Verification is measurable: (1) model availability latency against SLA, (2) feature coverage against agreed site feature list, (3) query response time for planning/execution consumers. SafetyAlert interface enables verification of hazard detection and deviation reporting against 'worksite safety plan and operational policies.'
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: EXCELLENT. FR allocation to NGHE Solution is unambiguous. NGHE owns the hybrid baseline-augmented model as single authoritative source. Responsibility boundaries are crisp: Vehicle/Teleoperation supply telemetry (input); Safety Services consume SafetyAlert (output); Planning/Execution consume Tasking queries (output). No ownership ambiguity or cross-cutting concerns detected.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 logically and completely addresses FR. DP specifies: (1) composite spatial representation via baseline anchoring + live augmentation (addresses 'capture and represent'); (2) multi-modal sensor fusion + deviation/hazard detection (addresses 'machine-readable model'); (3) unified geometric/semantic model with standardized query interface (addresses 'worksite spatial features'); (4) incremental updates and conflict resolution (addresses 'freshness and consistency'). DP is solution-complete and non-prescriptive about implementation packaging.

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: Telemetry ingestion and sensor fusion preprocessing. DP specifies 'multi-modal sensor observations' but does not clarify whether raw sensor data processing (filtering, decimation, coordinate transformation) occurs in Vehicle/Teleoperation or NGHE. If scattered across Vehicle instances, fusion logic becomes distributed and difficult to maintain. MITIGATION: Define explicit sensor preprocessing contract at Telemetry interface boundary; push edge filtering to Vehicle/Teleoperation to reduce NGHE load, but centralize fusion logic in NGHE.

HIGH: Baseline template synchronization across distributed NGHE instances. DP requires 'anchoring to pre-defined worksite templates' but does not specify how baseline consistency is maintained if NGHE is deployed redundantly. Network Link interface mentions 'synchronizes baseline templates' but lacks explicit conflict resolution policy. MITIGATION: Define strong consistency model (e.g., primary-replica, quorum-based) and publish conflict resolution rules aligned with safety constraints.

MEDIUM: Model freshness and incremental update strategy. DP states 'maintain model freshness through incremental updates' but does not specify update batching, versioning, or rollback semantics. If update logic is scattered across Telemetry ingestion, baseline synchronization, and conflict resolution, coherence risks emerge. MITIGATION: Centralize update state machine in NGHE; publish versioning and rollback policies.

MEDIUM: SafetyAlert latency and real-time guarantees. DP requires 'detect deviations and dynamic hazards' but does not specify detection latency or alert propagation SLA. If Safety Services depends on real-time alerts for constraint enforcement, NGHE model update latency becomes a safety-critical path. MITIGATION: Establish hard real-time SLA for hazard detection and alert publication; design NGHE with bounded latency guarantees.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution containment score of 0.92 is well-justified. Hybrid model ownership is appropriately centralized; diffusion score of 0.08 reflects only necessary cross-boundary dependencies (telemetry ingestion, safety constraint enforcement). No evidence of scattered DP logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. However, four diffusion risks identified above require explicit interface contracts and internal design decisions to prevent future diffusion as system evolves. Recommend documenting: (1) Telemetry preprocessing boundary, (2) baseline synchronization protocol, (3) incremental update state machine, (4) hazard detection latency SLA.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (DP refinement): Clarify sensor preprocessing boundary. Specify which transformations (filtering, decimation, coordinate alignment) occur in Vehicle/Teleoperation vs. NGHE. Recommend: raw sensor data ingestion at Telemetry interface; edge preprocessing in Vehicle/Teleoperation to reduce NGHE load; fusion and model integration in NGHE.

REVISION 2 (DP refinement): Define baseline template synchronization protocol. Specify consistency model (strong/eventual), conflict resolution rules, and versioning semantics for distributed NGHE deployments. Recommend: publish explicit conflict resolution policy aligned with safety constraints and operational priorities (e.g., 'safety-critical deviations override baseline updates').

REVISION 3 (DP refinement): Specify incremental update and versioning strategy. Define update batching, model versioning, rollback semantics, and consistency guarantees. Recommend: document update state machine and publish version history to Tasking consumers for planning/execution traceability.

REVISION 4 (IM interface refinement): Add latency SLA to SafetyAlert interface. Specify maximum detection latency for dynamic hazards and deviation alerts. Recommend: establish hard real-time guarantee (e.g., <100ms for safety-critical hazards) and design NGHE with bounded latency architecture (e.g., real-time kernel, priority queues, resource reservation).

REVISION 5 (acceptance criteria refinement): Extend acceptance criteria to include model freshness and consistency metrics. Recommend: add 'model update latency <X ms', 'baseline synchronization consistency >Y%', 'hazard detection latency <Z ms', and 'feature coverage >N% of agreed site features' as measurable acceptance gates.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, but the DP-IM gate identifies four diffusion risks of CRITICAL to MEDIUM severity that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Safety criticality is HIGH, making latency and consistency guarantees non-negotiable. The recommended revisions (1-5) are architectural clarifications that belong at the current level, not sub-FR decomposition. Revising DP and IM interface contracts now prevents scattered logic and safety-critical path ambiguities downstream. Once revisions are incorporated and re-gated, decomposition can proceed with confidence that lower-level FRs will inherit well-defined, unambiguous parent constraints.

revise.why: Five targeted revisions required: (1) DP refinement: clarify sensor preprocessing boundary (raw ingestion at Telemetry interface; edge filtering in Vehicle/Teleoperation; fusion in NGHE). (2) DP refinement: define baseline template synchronization protocol with explicit conflict resolution policy aligned to safety constraints (e.g., 'safety-critical deviations override baseline updates'). (3) DP refinement: specify incremental update state machine, versioning semantics, and rollback strategy to prevent scattered update logic. (4) IM interface refinement: add hard real-time SLA to SafetyAlert interface (recommend <100ms for safety-critical hazards; design NGHE with bounded latency guarantees). (5) Acceptance criteria refinement: extend to include model freshness (<X ms latency), baseline synchronization consistency (>Y%), hazard detection latency (<Z ms), and feature coverage (>N%) as measurable gates. These revisions are DP/IM-level decisions that clarify ownership, interface contracts, and real-time guarantees without requiring sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is deployed as a single authoritative instance or with strong consistency guarantees across replicas (assumption underlying baseline synchronization mitigation).

Vehicle/Teleoperation subsystem can perform edge preprocessing (filtering, decimation, coordinate transformation) without violating its own resource or latency constraints (assumption underlying sensor preprocessing boundary).
Safety Services can enforce real-time constraints if NGHE provides hazard detection alerts within <100ms (assumption underlying SafetyAlert latency SLA).
Planning/Execution functions can tolerate model update latency consistent with operational tempo (assumption underlying incremental update strategy).
Worksite baseline templates are stable enough to support strong consistency synchronization (assumption underlying baseline template protocol).
open_risks: CRITICAL: Telemetry ingestion and sensor fusion preprocessing. If raw sensor data processing is scattered across Vehicle instances, fusion logic becomes distributed and difficult to maintain. Mitigation: define explicit sensor preprocessing contract at Telemetry interface; push edge filtering to Vehicle/Teleoperation; centralize fusion in NGHE.

HIGH: Baseline template synchronization across distributed NGHE instances. If consistency model and conflict resolution policy are not explicitly defined, safety-critical deviations may be overridden by stale baseline updates. Mitigation: publish strong consistency model (primary-replica or quorum-based) and conflict resolution rules aligned to safety constraints.

MEDIUM: Model freshness and incremental update strategy. If update logic is scattered across telemetry ingestion, baseline synchronization, and conflict resolution, coherence risks emerge. Mitigation: centralize update state machine in NGHE; publish versioning and rollback policies.

MEDIUM: SafetyAlert latency and real-time guarantees. If hazard detection latency is not bounded, Safety Services may not be able to enforce real-time constraints. Mitigation: establish hard real-time SLA (<100ms for safety-critical hazards); design NGHE with bounded latency architecture (real-time kernel, priority queues, resource reservation).

MEDIUM: Acceptance criteria measurability. Current acceptance criteria lack quantitative thresholds for model freshness, consistency, and hazard detection latency. Mitigation: extend acceptance criteria to include model update latency <X ms, baseline synchronization consistency >Y%, hazard detection latency <Z ms, and feature coverage >N%.
evidence_needed: Sensor preprocessing boundary specification: document which transformations (filtering, decimation, coordinate alignment) occur in Vehicle/Teleoperation vs. NGHE; validate that edge preprocessing reduces NGHE load without compromising fusion accuracy.

Baseline template synchronization protocol: publish consistency model (strong/eventual), conflict resolution rules, and versioning semantics; validate against distributed deployment scenarios and safety constraint enforcement.

Incremental update state machine: document update batching, model versioning, rollback semantics, and consistency guarantees; validate that update logic is centralized in NGHE and not scattered across subsystems.

SafetyAlert latency SLA: establish hard real-time guarantee (e.g., <100ms for safety-critical hazards); validate NGHE architecture supports bounded latency (real-time kernel, priority queues, resource reservation); measure end-to-end latency from hazard detection to alert publication.

Acceptance criteria quantification: define measurable thresholds for model update latency, baseline synchronization consistency, hazard detection latency, and feature coverage; validate against operational requirements and safety constraints.
Distributed NGHE deployment validation: if NGHE is deployed redundantly, validate baseline synchronization protocol and conflict resolution under network partition and replica divergence scenarios.
